did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a hybrid deep learning framework for skin lesion segmentation and multi-class classification. The method integrates a U-Net-based segmentation network with an EfficientNet-B0 classification backbone, utilizes Grad-CAM for explainability, and was trained/evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'TYPE Original Research' and presents a new hybrid deep learning framework for skin lesion segmentation and classification. It details original methods (U-Net and EfficientNet-B0), experimental results on the HAM10000 dataset, and specific performance metrics (Dice coefficient, accuracy), distinguishing it from a review or survey.

title: An explainable hybrid deep learning framework for precise skin lesion segmentation and multi-class classification
brief_summary: This study addresses the diagnostic challenges of skin diseases by proposing an explainable hybrid deep learning framework for precise skin lesion segmentation and multi-class classification. The model integrates a U-Net-based segmentation network with an EfficientNet-B0 classification backbone and utilizes Grad-CAM to generate heatmaps for interpretability. The framework was trained and evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset to assess its performance in delineating lesions and predicting classes. The results demonstrated robust performance, achieving a Dice coefficient surpassing 0.85 for segmentation and classification accuracy nearing 85%. Despite challenges like class imbalance, the model provided reliable results across different skin conditions, highlighting the value of explainable AI in clinical settings.

all_task_types.details: The model uses a U-Net-based segmentation network to delineate skin lesion boundaries, achieving a Dice coefficient of 0.8622 and IoU of 0.7736 on the HAM10000 dataset.

The model performs multi-class classification of skin lesions into seven diagnostic categories (MEL, NV, BCC, AKIEC, BKL, DF, VASC) using an EfficientNet-B0 backbone, achieving an accuracy of approximately 85% (specifically 84.32% in results, 85.57% in table) and AUC-ROC of 0.8108.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A large, diverse public dataset of dermoscopic images containing detailed segmentation masks and seven diagnostic labels. It was obtained from Kaggle (https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/kmader/skin-cancer-mnist-ham10000).
classes_used: actinic keratoses and intraepithelial carcinoma / squamous cell carcinoma in situ (AKIEC)

use.training: Used for training the hybrid deep learning framework (U-Net segmentation and EfficientNet-B0 classification) with stratified data division and curriculum-guided training to address class imbalance.
use.validation: Used for validation (20% of the dataset, 2,003 images) during the training process.
use.testing: Used for testing (20% of the dataset, 2,003 images) to evaluate the model's performance on segmentation (Dice coefficient, IoU) and classification (accuracy, AUC-ROC).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To address class imbalance during the training process.
To address class imbalance and improve model robustness.

statistical_methods_used.method: Class-wise metric analysis (Precision, Recall, F1-score, Support)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate performance disparities across classes, particularly for minority classes like melanoma and dermatofibroma, noting that aggregated metrics can conceal poor performance on rare classes.

statistical_methods_used.method: Macro average and Weighted average calculations
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To provide comprehensive views of classification performance by balancing trade-offs between measures across all classes.
To quantitatively assess interpretability by comparing thresholded Grad-CAM maps against lesion boundaries (ground truth masks).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as Dice coefficient, IoU, accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC.

HAM10000.notes: The model achieved high precision and recall for NV but struggled with minority classes like DF and MEL. The segmentation branch showed strong results with Dice = 0.8622 and IoU = 0.7736. Grad-CAM IoU matched segmentation IoU at 0.7736. The text mentions classification accuracy nearing 85% and approaching 90% in different sections, with Table 2 reporting 0.843235 and section 4.1 stating 85.57%. Table 3 reports segmentation accuracy as 0.9338.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid deep learning framework combining U-Net-based segmentation network and EfficientNet-B0 classification backbone

architecture_details.segmentation_branch: U-Net architecture with ResNet34 encoder, utilizing multi-scale dilated convolutions and skip connections
architecture_details.classification_branch: EfficientNet-B0 backbone with global average pooling and dense layers with optional dropout regularization
architecture_details.shared_components: Encoder features are shared across both tasks

training_details.loss_function: Composite loss: Dice Loss for segmentation and Cross-Entropy Loss for classification
training_details.learning_rate: Constant learning rate (no specific value reported, typical range 1e-3–1e-5 mentioned)
training_details.regularization: No explicit weight decay; dropout in classification dense layers
training_details.training_strategy: Curriculum-guided training to address class imbalance

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Proposed Hybrid Deep Learning Framework (U-Net-based segmentation network with EfficientNet-B0 classification backbone)

Stratified sampling/splitting to maintain class distribution

conclusions: The proposed hybrid deep learning framework, integrating a U-Net-based segmentation network with an EfficientNet-B0 classification backbone, demonstrates robust performance for skin lesion segmentation and multi-class classification on the HAM10000 dataset.

The model achieved a Dice coefficient exceeding 0.85 (specifically 0.8622) for segmentation and a classification accuracy approaching 85-90% (reported as 84.32% in results and 85.57% in comparative tables), indicating competitive diagnostic support capabilities.

The integration of Grad-CAM for explainable AI (XAI) enhances transparency by generating heatmaps that align with clinically relevant lesion features, with a mean Intersection-over-Union (IoU) of 0.7736 between highlighted regions and ground truth masks.

While the model performs well on majority classes like benign nevi (NV), it exhibits lower recall and consistency for minority classes such as melanoma (MEL) and dermatofibroma (DF), highlighting the persistent challenge of class imbalance in dermatological datasets.
Aggregated metrics can conceal poor performance on rare but clinically critical classes; therefore, class-specific reporting is indispensable for evaluating diagnostic reliability.

The use of explainable AI is crucial for clinical acceptance, as it fosters trust by allowing clinicians to visualize the regions influencing model decisions, though these tools should be regarded as supportive rather than definitive diagnostic evidence.
Limitations include the model's exclusive training on the HAM10000 dataset, which may limit generalizability to diverse global skin types, imaging devices, and real-world multi-center scenarios.

Current saliency-based methods like Grad-CAM may produce class-agnostic artifacts or highlight non-causal regions, necessitating future research into clinician-in-the-loop validation and more robust quantitative measures of interpretability.
important_remarks: The study is limited by exclusive reliance on the HAM10000 dataset, which may restrict generalizability to diverse global skin types, imaging devices, and real-world multi-center clinical scenarios.

Significant class imbalance exists in the HAM10000 dataset, with benign nevi (NV) dominating; this leads to reduced performance for minority classes like melanoma (MEL) and dermatofibroma (DF), highlighting the risk of false negatives for malignant cases.
Aggregated metrics (e.g., overall accuracy) can conceal poor performance on rare but clinically critical classes; class-specific reporting is essential for evaluating diagnostic reliability.
The model's inference time exceeds 40 milliseconds per image, which is noted as practical for real-time diagnostic scenarios but requires consideration for deployment constraints.

Current saliency-based explainability methods like Grad-CAM may produce class-agnostic artifacts or highlight non-causal regions, necessitating future validation with clinicians and more robust interpretability measures.
The paper does not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; statistical significance of performance differences is not established.

Potential data leakage or bias issues are noted in related literature (e.g., Cassidy et al. regarding duplicate images across ISIC datasets), though the current study uses a standard stratified split on HAM10000 without explicit mention of duplicate removal.
The model was trained on a single GPU-enabled system with specific hardware specs (Intel i5-1135G7, 8GB RAM), which may limit reproducibility or performance in different computational environments.
